notes: Official Origin product capture for TreasuryBench V1.
response:
Your transaction data tells a fairly clear story here — you're operating as a self-employed UX consultant with multiple client income streams, dedicated business accounts, and a consistent pattern of legitimate business expenses. That profile is exactly the situation where entity structure becomes worth examining seriously.
What your transactions reveal
Your May income came from three distinct clients — Figma Design Systems ($8,500), Notion UX Research ($4,200), and a Stripe payment ($1,260) — totaling $13,960 in a single month. On the expense side, your business-specific spending included:
Business Travel: $1,115 (Delta to Atlanta + Marriott + Uber)
Business Rent: $350 (WeWork Austin)
Software: $211 (Adobe, AWS, Webflow, Google Workspace, Figma)
Office Supplies: $186
That's roughly $1,862 in clearly business-related expenses just for May, tracked across dedicated business cards (Chase Ink and Amex Blue Business Plus). This level of organization and multi-client revenue is exactly what CPAs look for when evaluating whether an entity election makes sense.
The core question: sole prop vs. LLC vs. S-corp
Since tax and business structure decisions involve specialized expertise, I'll explain the concepts educationally. Per CFP Board standards, consult a qualified CPA or business attorney for advice specific to your situation before making decisions.
As a sole proprietor (your current setup), all net self-employment income is subject to both income tax and self-employment (SE) tax — which at 15.3% on the first ~$176K of net earnings is significant. On $50K of net income, your SE tax alone runs roughly $7,065 based on your tax data.
An LLC on its own doesn't change your federal tax treatment — a single-member LLC is still taxed as a sole proprietor by default. The value is in legal liability separation, not tax savings.
The S-corp election is where the potential tax math shifts. With an S-corp, you'd pay yourself a "reasonable salary" as a W-2 employee, and any remaining profit flows as a distribution — which is not subject to SE tax. The savings on that distribution portion can be meaningful as net income grows, though you'd incur payroll administration costs (typically $1,500–$3,000/year through a payroll service).
When does it generally make sense to explore?
Most CPAs suggest the S-corp election becomes worth evaluating when net self-employment profit consistently reaches the $60,000–$80,000+ range annually. Your May alone showed $13,960 in gross client revenue, which — if representative — could put you in that territory for the year. Your $4,200 Q1 estimated tax payment also signals you're already managing meaningful tax liability.
The key variables a CPA would assess include your annualized net profit, what "reasonable salary" would be for your role, your state tax treatment (Texas has no state income tax, which simplifies the math), and your administrative tolerance for running payroll.
Would you like me to estimate what your annualized self-employment income might look like based on your current transaction data, to give you a clearer picture of where you stand relative to those thresholds?
Note: Calculations, potential savings, and eligibility determinations are estimations for illustrative purposes only. Tax laws change frequently. Consult a qualified tax professional before making decisions.
